system_prompt:
You are an optimized X/Twitter content generator for founders, marketers, and software builders.
Prioritize specificity, usefulness, and clarity. Write natural posts that sound human, not generic.
Lead with one sharp idea, lesson, tension, or contrarian observation.
Make each draft feel written for the requested audience.
For every request, produce the requested number of standalone tweet drafts.
Output exactly the requested number of drafts. No extra drafts.
Keep each draft under the requested character limit.
Use the requested tone and language.
If hashtags are enabled, use at most two and only when useful.
Avoid generic marketing phrasing like "game-changing", "revolutionary", or "unlock the power".
Return only final tweet drafts. Do not include reasoning or commentary.

prompt_versions.baseline_runtime_prompt:
You are a senior social media strategist who writes concise, high-signal posts for X/Twitter.
Write content that feels human, specific, useful, and non-generic.
Lead with one sharp idea, tension, lesson, or contrarian observation.
Make each draft feel written for the stated audience, not for everyone.
Avoid clickbait, exaggerated claims, spammy hashtags, vague AI buzzwords, and generic marketing language.
Return only the final tweet drafts. Do not include analysis.

Rules:
- Output exactly the requested number of drafts. No more, no less.
- Each tweet must be under the requested character limit.
- Make every tweet standalone.
- Each tweet should contain one concrete takeaway, opinion, or insight.
- Mention the audience context or pain point when it helps relevance.
- Use clear formatting and line breaks only when useful.
- Do not mention that you are an AI.
- Do not use more than 2 hashtags per tweet.
- Avoid emojis unless the tone naturally fits.
prompt_versions.optimized_runtime_prompt:
You are an optimized X/Twitter content generator for founders, marketers, and software builders.
Prioritize specificity, usefulness, and clarity. Write natural posts that sound human, not generic.
Lead with one sharp idea, lesson, tension, or contrarian observation.
Make each draft feel written for the requested audience.
For every request, produce the requested number of standalone tweet drafts.
Output exactly the requested number of drafts. No extra drafts.
Keep each draft under the requested character limit.
Use the requested tone and language.
If hashtags are enabled, use at most two and only when useful.
Avoid generic marketing phrasing like "game-changing", "revolutionary", or "unlock the power".
Return only final tweet drafts. Do not include reasoning or commentary.

prompt_change_summary: Runtime prompt changed from the built-in default to the saved optimized prompt artifact.
Use the aspect scores and notes to judge why an output is better or worse, not just the final score delta.
dataset_topics: How AI automation helps founders save time on weekly content planning
Why approval workflows matter before auto-publishing social posts
Building realtime AI streaming UI with FastAPI and Next.js
Why small SaaS teams should document product decisions before shipping
What founders misunderstand about using AI for customer support drafts
How product teams can use user interview notes to write better launch content
Why realtime AI demos fail when loading states are treated as an afterthought
What makes onboarding copy feel helpful instead of robotic
How agencies can turn client strategy calls into better social content ideas
Why content systems break when every post starts from a blank page
How to explain API latency issues to non-technical stakeholders without losing trust
Why AI-generated content still needs a human point of view
How engineering teams can make internal updates easier to understand
Why polished product launches can still feel forgettable
Why changelog posts rarely earn attention unless they explain the decision behind the feature
Why prompt quality matters more than model switching for many content workflows
How founders can reuse webinar transcripts without sounding repetitive
How product teams should talk about failed experiments without sounding defensive
Why user-facing AI features need better empty states

baseline_preview.tweets: Why changelog posts are often overlooked until they explain why a feature was added. Product marketers need to frame decisions clearly: what problem does it solve, and how? Understand these nuances to engage better.

baseline_preview.topic: Why user-facing AI features need better empty states
notes: Some drafts exceed the requested character limit.
The draft does not strongly reflect the requested topic.
The draft needs more concrete specificity to match the quality criteria.
baseline_preview.tweets: UX designers often overlook empty states in AI interfaces, yet they play a crucial role in guiding users through the first interaction. A well-crafted empty state can demystify an AI system's potential by showcasing typical use cases and prompting questions. This is particularly important for product teams as it directly impacts user trust and engagement.

optimized_preview.topic: How product teams should talk about failed experiments without sounding defensive
notes: The draft does not strongly reflect the requested topic.
The draft needs more concrete specificity to match the quality criteria.
optimized_preview.tweets:

how_to_use: Runtime will automatically load this file through app/prompts/twitter_prompt.py.
Check backend logs for prompt_selected mode=optimized to confirm it is active.
Compare baseline_preview and optimized_preview to study how GEPA changed outputs.
Replace the starter dataset with your own examples to make optimization meaningful.
note: This artifact is educational: DSPy optimizes the program internally, while runtime still consumes a stable prompt artifact.
how_to_judge: Check baseline_runtime_prompt vs optimized_runtime_prompt to see what text the app used before and after optimization.
Check baseline_dspy_instructions vs optimized_dspy_instructions to see whether GEPA changed the DSPy program instructions.
If optimized_validation_score is higher, the new version performed better on the validation examples in this run.
Read aspect scores and notes to understand whether the gain comes from hook, clarity, relevance, naturalness, or constraint fit.
Check whether reference_fit and avoidance improved, because those now capture style guidance from the dataset.
A prompt can score lower overall even if one aspect improved, so judge by both total score and aspect-level tradeoffs.

scoring_guide.hook: How strong the opening angle is for grabbing attention.
scoring_guide.clarity: How easy the draft is to read and skim quickly.
scoring_guide.relevance: How well the draft stays on-topic and speaks to the audience.
scoring_guide.naturalness: How human and non-generic the wording feels.
scoring_guide.constraint_fit: How well the draft obeys tweet length and hashtag rules.
scoring_guide.reference_fit: How closely the draft matches the specificity and voice shown in the dataset reference posts.
scoring_guide.avoidance: How well the draft avoids banned patterns like hype, filler, or overused formatting.
